species: Woodpecker + River + Frog Polyrhythm -- three independent rhythmic pulses, riparian old-growth, spring
scientific_name: Dryocopus pileatus (Pileated Woodpecker, Picidae) + river current percussion (abiotic) + Pseudacris regilla (Pacific Tree Frog / Pacific Chorus Frog, Hylidae)
sound_type: Three independent rhythmic streams, zero synchronization, zero shared meter. (1) Pileated Woodpecker drumming: roll rate 17-25 Hz within each sequence; sequence rate 10-15/min = 0.17-0.25 Hz; sequence duration 1-2 sec; pause 5-15 sec; amplitude 75-95 dB at 30m; peak energy 500-800 Hz wood resonance; mechanism: biological motor pattern. (2) River current standing wave oscillation: 0.5-3 Hz (hydraulically determined, continuously modulated by discharge); broadband turbulence continuous; discrete aperiodic percussion events (rolling rocks, debris impact); amplitude 60-85 dB; mechanism: fluid dynamics. (3) Pacific Tree Frog chorus AM rate: 1-3 Hz (temperature-dependent, individual call rate 40-80 calls/min, chorus AM from unsynchronized individuals); individual call frequency 2000-3500 Hz; amplitude 80-100 dB at 10m; mechanism: temperature-dependent neural oscillation. No coupling between streams. Three independent physical mechanisms. THE MOST RHYTHMICALLY COMPLEX NATURAL SOUND ENTRY IN THE SPS ENGINE.
ecosystem: Riparian old-growth. Pacific Northwest river corridor with: (1) large-diameter dead snags (50+ cm) for Pileated drumming -- old-growth indicator; (2) intact mountain watershed hydrology for spring snowmelt river discharge; (3) floodplain shallow water from actively flooding spring river for frog breeding. Channelized or logged riparian loses one or more components. The polyrhythm = ecological health certificate for intact riparian old-growth.
season: Spring, April-May coincidence window. Pileated drumming peaks March-June (April-May maximum breeding activity). River discharge peaks April-May Pacific Northwest mountain rivers (snowmelt). Pacific Tree Frog chorus peaks when water temperature reaches 10-15°C (April-May riparian zone). Spring phenological pulse: biological reproduction + hydrological maximum + thermal conditions align for 3-6 week window.
family: Multi-species. Dryocopus pileatus (Order Piciformes, Family Picidae). Pseudacris regilla (Order Anura, Family Hylidae). Plus abiotic acoustic source (river current, no taxonomy).
milestone: THE MOST RHYTHMICALLY COMPLEX NATURAL SOUND ENTRY IN THE SPS ENGINE. Three independent rhythmic streams at different tempos (woodpecker 0.17-0.25 Hz, river 0.5-3 Hz, frog 1-3 Hz) with zero correlation and zero shared mechanism. E=9 through simultaneous rhythmic complexity rather than amplitude alone. Pileated Woodpecker as old-growth indicator: requires 50+ cm diameter snags. Pacific Tree Frog 'ribbit' sound: the source of the generic frog sound used in film and popular culture (recorded in California, used globally). Riparian old-growth ecological health certificate: all three streams present simultaneously = intact watershed, intact forest, intact floodplain.

coordinates.description: Representative: Skykomish River valley, King/Snohomish County WA. 47.98°N 121.55°W. Old-growth riparian with Pileated Woodpecker habitat, mountain spring discharge, Pacific Tree Frog breeding habitat. Also: Hoh River valley (Olympic NP), North Fork Stillaguamish, Elwha River (post-dam removal restoration).

acoustic_data.stream_1_woodpecker: Dryocopus pileatus. Roll rate: 17-25 strikes/sec (17-25 Hz). Sequence rate: 10-15/min (0.17-0.25 Hz). Sequence duration: 1-2 sec. Pause: 5-15 sec. Amplitude: 75-95 dB at 30m. Frequency: peak 500-800 Hz (wood resonance), extends 100-2000 Hz. Mechanism: biological motor pattern (neurologically determined). Snag selection: 50+ cm diameter dead wood for resonance.
acoustic_data.stream_2_river: Standing wave oscillation: 0.5-3 Hz (hydraulically determined by water velocity, obstacle geometry, water depth). Continuously modulated by discharge. Broadband turbulence: continuous, covers 20-10000 Hz. Discrete events: rolling rocks (low-freq impulsive), debris impact (wideband transient). Amplitude: 60-85 dB (discharge-dependent). Spring maximum: April-May snowmelt peak.
acoustic_data.stream_3_frog: Pseudacris regilla. Individual call rate: 40/min at 10°C to 80+/min at 20°C. Q10 (temperature coefficient) ~2-3 for call rate. Chorus AM rate: 1-3 Hz (unsynchronized individual calls creating amplitude modulation). Individual call frequency: 2000-3500 Hz fundamental, harmonics to 6000+ Hz. Amplitude: 80-100 dB at 10m. Mechanism: temperature-dependent neural oscillation.
acoustic_data.polyrhythm_analysis: All three streams operate at approximately 0.2-3 Hz range but driven by different mechanisms: biological motor (woodpecker), fluid dynamics (river), neural oscillation (frog). Zero correlation. No coupling. The three streams exist simultaneously by spatial coincidence (woodpecker in snag, river in channel, frogs in shallow) -- not by any biological or physical interaction. Maximum natural rhythmic complexity.
acoustic_data.e9_calibration: E=9 through simultaneous rhythmic complexity. Individual streams: woodpecker E=6-7, river E=7-8, frog E=6-7. Combined simultaneous at spring maximum: E=9. The brain cannot simplify the polyrhythm into a single pattern; inability to resolve rhythmic relationships creates acoustic overload proportionate to E=9. Same E=9 mechanism as Botch (S36-332): independent rhythmic complexity at maximum expression, not amplitude alone.

nodes.title: Three-Stream Natural Polyrhythm -- Most Rhythmically Complex SPS Entry
nodes.description: Three independent rhythmic streams (woodpecker biological motor, river hydraulics, frog neural oscillation), all operating at approximately 0.2-3 Hz, zero correlation, zero coupling, zero shared mechanism. Maximum natural rhythmic complexity. E=9 through complexity rather than amplitude alone.

nodes.title: Spring Phenological Pulse -- April-May Simultaneous Peak
nodes.description: Three independently timed phenomena peak simultaneously: Pileated breeding season (biological spring trigger), snowmelt discharge (hydrological spring maximum), Pacific Tree Frog breeding chorus (thermal spring trigger at 10-15°C). 3-6 week window of simultaneous maximum. The polyrhythm only exists at full expression during this spring window.

nodes.title: Riparian Old-Growth Health Certificate
nodes.description: All three streams require specific ecological conditions: snags (50+ cm, old-growth/mature forest), intact watershed hydrology, floodplain shallow water. All three present simultaneously = riparian old-growth intact. Channelized river loses frog; logged riparian loses woodpecker; dammed river loses discharge pulse. The polyrhythm is an ecological diagnostic.

nodes.title: Pileated Woodpecker as Old-Growth Indicator Species
nodes.description: Dryocopus pileatus requires 50+ cm diameter snags for drumming and foraging (carpenter ants in dead wood). These snags are produced only by old-growth or very mature second-growth. Pileated presence implies mature forest age. The woodpecker's drum certifies the forest's age. The instrument requires the ecosystem.

nodes.title: Pseudacris regilla -- The Source of 'Ribbit'
nodes.description: The Pacific Tree Frog is the source of the ribbit sound used in films and popular culture as the generic frog sound. Recorded in California for film, used globally. Most people know the call without knowing its source. The world's most widely heard Pacific Northwest wildlife sound, unattributed.

cross_references.relationship: E=9 + E=9 = E=18. Botch mathcore: multiple simultaneous irregular time signatures, maximum musical rhythmic complexity. Woodpecker + river + frog: three independent natural rhythmic streams, zero shared meter, maximum natural rhythmic complexity. Both defined by simultaneous independent rhythmic pulses that do not share a common meter. The engine's most rhythmically complex music paired with the engine's most rhythmically complex natural sound.

Both E=9 in spring riparian ecosystems. Stonefly hatch: E=9 through mass biological scale (thousands of individuals). Woodpecker + river + frog: E=9 through rhythmic complexity. Same energy, different mechanism. Arc opens (329) and closes (332) at E=9 through different spring riparian mechanisms.

Three biological layers at E=7. Three natural rhythmic streams (including frog) at E=9. Both multi-source spring events with amphibian component. The polyrhythm surpasses the chorus by adding the river (abiotic) and woodpecker (non-chorus biological rhythm) and adding rhythmic independence as the organizing principle.
